rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the policy text: it proposes regulatory energy-efficiency action for appliances. Verbatim text includes: 'consider setting a minimum energy efficiency requirement so that certain appliances must meet the minimum energy efficiency standards before they can be supplied in Hong Kong' and 'consider expanding the scope of the scheme to cover non-domestic or commercial appliances.' These measures reduce electricity use (as also reflected in the canonical mechanism 'appliance_energy_efficiency_standards_and_labeling → electricity_use_reduction'), which is a standard Mitigation pathway even if the passage lacks an explicit emissions/mitigation token. The parent statement also targets cutting building electricity consumption. With climate_screen marked 'explicit_self', the row should remain in the climate dataset with primary category Mitigation unchanged.
policy_statement: The Government will keep the Mandatory Energy Efficiency Labelling Scheme (MEELS) under review, consider setting minimum energy efficiency requirements for specified appliances before supply in Hong Kong, and consider expanding MEELS to non-domestic/commercial appliances.
mechanism_description: The government will keep the appliance efficiency labelling scheme under review and consider expanding it and setting minimum efficiency standards so appliances use less electricity, reducing emissions from electricity generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Same appliance service → ↓ electricity input → ↓ fossil electricity generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Appliance manufacturers/retailers and consumers shift toward higher-efficiency models due to mandatory minimum energy performance standards (MEPS) and/or energy labeling that influences purchasing.  
2) Mediating physical process: Higher efficiency lowers kWh required to deliver the same service, reducing aggregate electricity load on the grid and therefore reducing operation of marginal (often fossil) generators.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced CO₂ (and other pollutant) emissions from electricity generation, with magnitude dependent on grid emissions intensity and appliance stock turnover.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lower electricity demand from appliances, leading to reduced power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Appliance efficiency policies reduce electricity use; lower electricity generation reduces GHG emissions—this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “resource efficiency” language can apply (less energy per service), the dominant climate outcome across contexts is emissions avoidance via reduced power-sector generation, which fits Mitigation best.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is near-zero carbon, emissions benefits diminish, but the intended and typical climate-relevant effect remains emissions reduction in most jurisdictions; rebound effects may partially offset savings but do not change the primary mechanism.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that sets/strengthens appliance labels or efficiency standards aims to cut appliance electricity consumption, which (where electricity has nonzero emissions) reduces GHGs—consistent across residential and commercial appliances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy is framed as standards vs labeling, mandatory vs voluntary, and which appliance classes are covered is policy-specific and requires policy-level evidence; only the primary category is assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions reductions depend on grid carbon intensity and enforcement/compliance; rebound effects (greater usage due to lower operating cost) can reduce net savings. If applied to gas appliances (not this stated mechanism) the pathway would shift to direct fuel combustion reductions.
key_indicators: “Mandatory Energy Efficiency Labelling Scheme (MEELS)”; “consider setting a minimum energy efficiency requirement”; “must meet the minimum energy efficiency standards before they can be supplied”; “upgrading the grading standards”; “expanding the scope… to cover non-domestic or commercial appliances”
co_benefits: Reduced electricity bills for consumers/businesses; improved air quality from lower power generation emissions; reduced peak demand/greater grid reliability.
instance_edge_case_notes: Both labeling (information-disclosure) and minimum requirements (MEPS) are mentioned; classified by the more binding element (“minimum energy efficiency requirement… before they can be supplied”). Also partly forward-looking (“will consider”), but still describes the intended instrument type.
